characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.description: The story's protagonist. A mean, hostile, unflinchingly miserly old Victorian businessman who detests Christmas and the merriment it brings out in others. Over the course of the story, he becomes much kinder. Abusive Parents: Well, abusiveuncleto his nephew Fred. In hisBad Future, it's implied that Fred didn't mourn him when he diednotealthough all we hear about Fred is that he manfully consoled Bob and offered to help, rather than talk about his own grief. Sadly, it is implied that hebecame exactly like his neglectful and cruel fatherwho sent him to a boarding school to get rid of him and chose to leave him there even when everyone else was on Christmas vacation. Actually Pretty Funny: Took his nephew's jokes about him at his Christmas party surprisingly well, only lamenting he wasn't there to hear it and laugh with everyone else. Adaptational Jerkass:Many adaptations have ScroogeKick the Doga lot more often, such as denying Bob Cratchit pay on Christmas for being absent, being actively cruel towards Tiny Tim, having his greed exaggerated into out-and-out vanity, and overall being more of an openly callousJerkassthan the quiet and miserable loner he is by tradition.Also common in many versions of the play is that Scrooge'sHeel–Face Turndoesn't happen until the end of the third vision; in the book, however, merely being shown the Christmas present—the joy his nephew has and the prospect of Tiny Tim's death—is enough for him to start regretting his earlier greed. This change has the unintended effect of making it look like Scrooge was only motivated out of self-interest, rather than genuine compassion. Many adaptations have ScroogeKick the Doga lot more often, such as denying Bob Cratchit pay on Christmas for being absent, being actively cruel towards Tiny Tim, having his greed exaggerated into out-and-out vanity, and overall being more of an openly callousJerkassthan the quiet and miserable loner he is by tradition.

characters.name: Ebenezer Scrooge
characters.description: The story's protagonist. A mean, hostile, unflinchingly miserly old Victorian businessman who detests Christmas and the merriment it brings out in others. Over the course of the story, he becomes much kinder. Abusive Parents: Well, abusiveuncleto his nephew Fred. In hisBad Future, it's implied that Fred didn't mourn him when he diednotealthough all we hear about Fred is that he manfully consoled Bob and offered to help, rather than talk about his own grief. Sadly, it is implied that hebecame exactly like his neglectful and cruel fatherwho sent him to a boarding school to get rid of him and chose to leave him there even when everyone else was on Christmas vacation. Actually Pretty Funny: Took his nephew's jokes about him at his Christmas party surprisingly well, only lamenting he wasn't there to hear it and laugh with everyone else. Adaptational Jerkass:Many adaptations have ScroogeKick the Doga lot more often, such as denying Bob Cratchit pay on Christmas for being absent, being actively cruel towards Tiny Tim, having his greed exaggerated into out-and-out vanity, and overall being more of an openly callousJerkassthan the quiet and miserable loner he is by tradition.Also common in many versions of the play is that Scrooge'sHeel–Face Turndoesn't happen until the end of the third vision; in the book, however, merely being shown the Christmas present—the joy his nephew has and the prospect of Tiny Tim's death—is enough for him to start regretting his earlier greed. This change has the unintended effect of making it look like Scrooge was only motivated out of self-interest, rather than genuine compassion. Many adaptations have ScroogeKick the Doga lot more often, such as denying Bob Cratchit pay on Christmas for being absent, being actively cruel towards Tiny Tim, having his greed exaggerated into out-and-out vanity, and overall being more of an openly callousJerkassthan the quiet and miserable loner he is by tradition.

characters.name: Bob Cratchit
characters.description: Scrooge's hardworking and cheerful clark and bookkeeper, and a loving husband and father of four. Broken Tears: In theBad Futuresegment, he cries his eyes out over the death of Tiny Tim. Full-Name Basis: He's usually referred by his full name by those discussing the story. Good Parents: He adores his children and the feeling is mutual. Happily Married: Loves his wife and the feeling is mutual.

characters.name: Tiny Tim
characters.description: Bob Cratchit's youngest child, afflicted with an unidentified illness that renders him lame. Death of a Child: A little boy with an illness that has not only crippled him but is slowlykilling him. In the future where Scrooge never redeemed himself, poor Tim doesn't get the medical help he needs and dies very young. Delicate and Sickly: He's suffering from an unspecified illness that has already crippled his leg and will kill him unless his family can afford medical treatment. Littlest Cancer Patient: His illness is never explicitly identified, but the trope still fits, being a pitiful young boy with a possibly-fatal illness. One theory is polio due to his having to use a crutch to walk; other popular theories are tuberculosis, rickets, or renal tubular acidosis. Whatever it is, it's at least treatable; his primary obstacle to good health is his family's poverty rather than the medical advances of the era. Morality Pet: To Scrooge. The first time he begins to regret his "Kill the Poor" mentality is when he sees him.

characters.name: Nephew Fred
characters.description: Scrooge's only living relative by his sister, who never ceases trying to bring out the good in his uncle. Determinator: Fred has never stopped inviting his uncle to Christmas dinner even though he never shows up. His invitations do get answered in the end once Scrooge has a genuine change of heart. Everyone Has Standards: Although he isn't above the occasional jab at his uncle's expense, when someone else makes a particularly mean-spirited joke about Scrooge, Fred immediately tells them off. Scrooge may be a bad person for most of the story, but he's stillfamily. Flat Character: Subverted. Most of Fred's purpose in the story is to be Scrooge's bridge back into human relations as he is his only living relative. He also exists to show that when Scrooge isolates himself in his horrible little apartment on Christmas, he is actually making a choice to be alone rather than forced to be that way by circumstance. With that said, while his role in the story is mostly to compliment Scrooge's arc, Fred does still have a clear character. He's Scrooge's polar opposite (he's optimistic, cheerful, kind-hearted, joyful, romantic, generous, and someone who adores Christmas) but can still throw a few jabs at his uncle when he's not around to scold him for his isolated and miserly life (which is understandable, as Scrooge keeps turning down his offer to join him for Christmas dinner despite the fact he has kept asking him for who knows how many years). Happily Married: He has a very loving relationship with his wife.

characters.name: Portly Gentlemen
characters.description: Two charity workers attempting to collect donations for the poor because of Christmastime. Adaptational Slimness: There are some versions of the story (ex.the Muppets version, where they are played by Bunsen and Beaker) that make one of them thin so that they can serve as aFat and Skinnyduo. Good Samaritan: Their main character trait is soliciting aid for the poor. No Name Given: They're not given names and the narration never calls them anything other than "the portly gentlemen". Those Two Guys: There are two of them and they only partially affect the plot.

characters.name: Mrs. Dilber
characters.description: Adaptational Villainy: In the Mr. Magoo version, right down to leading Old Joe, the Undertaker, and the Laundress in a hilariousVillain Song. Composite Character: Many adaptations composite her with Scrooge's unnamed housekeeper who stole his bed curtains, sheets, and shirt, while in the book she was the laundress who stole his sugar tongs, boots, and spoons. Demoted to Extra: In most adaptations, she's almost certain to show up in the Christmas Future scenes, but they'll rarely mention that she's Scrooge's laundress. The Help Helping Themselves: She happily pawns some of Scrooge's possessions after his death and is implied to have stolen from him during his life as well. No Sympathy: A rare sympathetic example. In Scrooge'sBad Future, she shows no remorse in pawning her boss's belongings after he dies. Given what Scrooge was like before his redemption, can you really blame her?

characters.name: Jacob Marley
characters.description: Scrooge's old business partner and his closest confidant. He is long dead by the start of the story, but that doesn't stop him from visiting Scrooge as a ghost to warn him of his impending fate as a revenant. Adaptational Alternate Ending: The 2006 animated film has him freed from his damnation since he redeemed himself by saving Scrooge from suffering the same fate. And I Must Scream: Marley is forced to carry the burden of his greed for all of eternity,Forced to Watchboth the suffering that he benefited from in life, and all the surplus sorrow that he can never help now. Though he's briefly permitted a chance to help Scrooge (and by extension, the whole Cratchit family and all of London) with his warning. Barred from the Afterlife: As punishment for his avarice, he is forced to wear heavy chains attached to money boxes and wander the Earth witnessing the consequences of his actions. All while being forbidden from ever moving on to the other side. Brutal Honesty: His famous line when Scrooge asks for comfort, "I have none to give". Though notcompletelyhonest, as his offer of redemption should be comforting, he has no comfort for Scrooge as he currently lives.

characters.name: The Wandering Spirits
characters.description: Adapted Out: They don't seem to appear in many adaptations ofA Christmas Carol. And I Must Scream: It is quite haunting and sad to see them floating around the town trying to do what they did not in life, neither seen nor heard by those whom they want so desperately to help but no longer can. Barred from the Afterlife: They are forbidden from ever moving on to the afterlife and are instead stuck among the living wearing chains symbolizing their greed. Chained by Fashion: They, like Marley, are often portrayed as being forever chained to weights, safes, etc. Heel–Face Door-Slam:Perpetually.They try to interfere for good in human affairs, but have lost their power to do so forever.

characters.name: Ghost of Christmas Past
characters.description: The first spirit, who shows Scrooge visions of his past Christmases. Alien Geometries: Itsbodyis comprised of this trope, leading it to arguably become the most vulnerable of the three ghosts toAdaptational Species Changesin adaptations."... its belt sparkled and glittered now in one part and now in another, and what was light one instant, at another time was dark, so the figure itself fluctuated in its distinctness: being now a thing with one arm, now with one leg, now with twenty legs, now a pair of legs without a head, now a head without a body: of which dissolving parts, no outline would be visible in the dense gloom wherein they melted away. And in the very wonder of this, it would be itself again; distinct and clear as ever." Ambiguous Gender: Hence why it's referred to as an "it". Adaptations usually avert this, usually by portraying it as a woman or sometimes a little girl, but there are others that keep the trope in—the Mr. Magoo version (which also has Past appearafterPresent for some reason) is a good example, where the ghost is portrayed as a young child with long hair and a tunic, but there's no indication as to whether it's male or female. Creepy Child: In some adaptations, it's portrayed as a ghostly child. It's notalwayscreepy, but more often than not it is. Cute Ghost Girl: Depicted as a cute female in some of the adaptations.

characters.name: Ghost of Christmas Present
characters.description: The second spirit, who shows Scrooge a vision of the Christmas that the morning will bring. Adaptational Curves: In the book, especially the illustrations, he's depicted as rather muscular. In many adaptations, he's made more plump, probably to make theSanta Clausconnection a bit more obvious. Adaptational Nice Guy: Several adaptations downplay his harshness to Scrooge's flaws to focus on his jovial idealism turning Scrooge around (especially since his bluntest lesson via Ignorance and Want is oftenAdapted Out). Especially evident inThe Muppet Christmas Carol, where he echoes Scrooge's cruelty only once, and has a much gentler and more encouraging goodbye, which genuinely saddens Scrooge due to having formed a legitimate bond with him. Beware the Nice Ones: He's the friendliest of the ghosts, but he's also the one who throws Scrooge's unkind wordsback at him, and exposes him to Ignorance and Want, which is what really begins to rattle Scrooge. Big Eater: Implied with the massive amount of food he's surrounded by when Scrooge first meets him.

characters.name: Ignorance and Want
characters.description: Two childlike creatures that hide underneath the Ghost of Christmas Present's cloak. Adaptation Expansion: Their appearance in the 2009 film has age to adulthood as The Spirit of Christmas Present dies, showing the end result of Scrooge's line of thinking - Ignorance becomes a knife-wielding thief who is locked in prison, while Want is implied to become a prostitute and is trapped in a straight-jacket, alluding to how woman of said profession were often thrown in asylums. Adapted Out: Frequently left out of adaptations, presumably because they're considered "too scary". This wouldn't be so bad if it weren't for the fact that they were valuable to Scrooge's redemption. Creepy Child: Both of them are silent, wraithlike creatures that resemble children and cling to the Ghost of Christmas Present. Creepy Twins: Possibly, but it's never elaborated on. Many adaptations that include them seem to invoke this idea by having them look similar to each other, though.

characters.name: Ghost of Christmas Yet To Come
characters.description: The third spirit, which shows Scrooge visions of a Christmas that may yet come to pass. Adaptational Nice Guy: Downplayed, but inthe Muppet adaptation, it comes off as friendlier toward Scrooge. It even puts its arm around him a few times. Adaptational Species Change: Of sorts—inone version, it's depicted as a mynah bird. Adaptational Villainy: Happens on occasion, due to it being the most menacing of the spirits. Both of the Disney versions are probably the best examples, wherein the spirit straight up tosses Ebenezer intoHelland in one versionlaughs mockingly at himas he desperately tries to scramble back up.Downplayed,though, as his core intention has always remained toscare Scrooge into changing for the better rather than actually harm him. Ambiguous Gender: Since it wears a face-concealing cloak and never speaks, it's not clear whether this spirit is supposed to be male, female, or even have a binary gender.

characters.name: Fan
characters.description: Scrooge's dearly beloved sister. Cool Big Sis: Scrooge's kind sister. Her death very badly affected him. Inverted, as she's much younger than Scrooge, though some adaptations make her older. Death by Childbirth: The book never says how she died, but several adaptations reveal that she died after giving birth to Fred. In these adaptations, this is why Scroogedislikes his nephew. Depending on the Writer: The adaptations vary on whether Fan was older or younger than her brother Scrooge. Nice Girl: Fan was a kind-hearted and sweet person.

characters.name: Mr. Fezziwig
characters.description: Scrooge's old employer, to whom he served under as an apprentice, who was known for his generosity and his vibrant Christmas parties. Benevolent Boss: He was a great boss to Scrooge and Dick Wilkins, his two apprentices. Scrooge admits he couldn't have worked for a kinder man. Big Fun: And how! He could give Fred's Christmas spirit a run for his money. Boisterous Bruiser: A big guy with a huge amount of energy. Cool Old Guy: Scrooge certainly thought he was a swell guy.

characters.name: Belle
characters.description: The only woman Scrooge ever loved, whom he failed to court successfully after he drifted away from her and became more focused on his business. Ascended Extra:Appears in a more prominent role in the 2001 version and gets a backstory (her father was a coachman who drank, and Scrooge met her because she was friends with Fan).In general, most adaptations show her attending Fezziwig’s ball while the book has no mention of her being present at the ball.InScrooged, her counterpart, Claire, is a much bigger consistent throughout the film that the Scrooge character still talks to in the present. An added stake to theBad Futureis himrealising his influence will corrupt her to be as cold-hearted as he was. The ending also has the two reconcile and get back together. Appears in a more prominent role in the 2001 version and gets a backstory (her father was a coachman who drank, and Scrooge met her because she was friends with Fan). In general, most adaptations show her attending Fezziwig’s ball while the book has no mention of her being present at the ball. InScrooged, her counterpart, Claire, is a much bigger consistent throughout the film that the Scrooge character still talks to in the present. An added stake to theBad Futureis himrealising his influence will corrupt her to be as cold-hearted as he was. The ending also has the two reconcile and get back together.

characters.name: Old Joe
characters.description: A street thug who buys stolen goods. Adaptation Expansion: In the 2001 adaptation, he is depicted as a sort of minion for Scrooge, who hires him to go around, harassing people who owe Scrooge debt. Animal Motifs: Inthe Muppet adaptation, he's represented by aGiant Spider. Ascended Extra: Appears in the present time as well in the 2001 adaptation. Card-Carrying Villain: The most unambiguously evil character in the story — although still pretty small beer next to the likes of Fagin or Bill Sykes, for a start.
